description: Project cleanup workflow: 1) Create new branch. 2) Ask user for main theorems (keep as-is) and secondary theorems (keep but may generalize). 3) Work down dependencies removing defs/lemmas not needed for these theorems. 4) Create NEW folder for cleaned files - do NOT modify originals until cleanup is complete. 5) Copy main theorems into separate files first, then add dependencies one lemma/def at a time until they compile. 6) Keep files under 1000 lines - split into subfolders with imports as needed. 7) Never copy huge chunks - go lemma by lemma, def by def. 8) Only delete/replace original files at the very end when everything compiles.

When moving definitions into clean files, ALWAYS check if mathlib already has an equivalent. E.g. use ModularGroup.fd instead of redefining fundamentalDomain. If replacing a def with a mathlib one, update all lemmas to use the new def as they are migrated.

KEY GOAL: Make as little new API as possible. Reuse mathlib definitions everywhere. If needed, build new API/lemmas FOR mathlib's existing definitions rather than creating custom ones. This applies to all defs: curves, integrals, winding numbers, residues, etc.

Cleanup should be planned so that multiple agents can work in parallel. Structure files and tasks so independent pieces can be cleaned concurrently. This is a HARD task - invest effort to get it right. Plan the dependency DAG of clean files so that once foundation files compile, multiple agents can work on independent branches simultaneously.

before_code: abbrev modularFormCompOfComplex (f : ModularForm (Gamma 1) k) : ℂ → ℂ := f ∘ UpperHalfPlane.ofComplex
after_code:
-- Use short notation instead of a definition:
local notation "f_ℂ" => (f : ModularForm (Gamma 1) k) ∘ UpperHalfPlane.ofComplex
description: Prefer notation over definitions for simple compositions like modularFormCompOfComplex (= f ∘ UpperHalfPlane.ofComplex). Using notation instead of a def means we don't need to unfold/simp the definition everywhere. This reduces API surface and makes proofs cleaner since the notation is transparent.

before_code:
theorem fd_eq_fd' : (𝒟 : Set ℍ) = 𝒟' := by ...
theorem S0_mem_fd_clean : ... := by rw [fd_eq_fd']; ...
after_code:
-- NO bridge theorems. Update ALL code to use ModularGroup.fd (𝒟) directly.
-- Delete custom fundamentalDomain and rewrite every lemma that used it.
description: When a definition we made is already in mathlib, do NOT create bridge theorems (like fd_eq_fd') between them. Instead, update ALL the code to use the mathlib definition directly. No bridges between fundamental domain definitions or any other duplicated defs. Full migration, not bridging.
Nothing should be in a file with more than 1000 lines. Split into separate files/subfolders as needed. This is a hard limit, not a suggestion.

The generalized residue theorem and winding number results should be KEPT in the cleanup. They are secondary theorems worth preserving. They should go in their own separate files/folders, not lumped in with other code.

before_code:
-- The main theorem is already available from the import.
-- `valenceFormula_textbook_orbit_finsum :
--     (orderAtCusp' f : ℂ) + ...`
after_code:
-- Put the actual theorem UNCOMMENTED in the file:
theorem valenceFormula_textbook_orbit_finsum ... := ...
-- Then bring in whatever results it needs to compile,
-- creating new files/imports as necessary.
description: NEVER put theorems as comments in a file! When moving a theorem into its own file, put the actual theorem statement UNCOMMENTED and then bring in whatever results it needs to fix any compilation errors. This may include starting new files and adding them as imports. A file with only commented-out theorem statements is COMPLETELY USELESS and must NEVER be done. Always do the real work of making the theorem compile.

When doing cleanups, be the OPPOSITE of lazy! No task is too big. NEVER simplify or shortcut the task. The harder the task, the more willing you should be to do it if it will make the file closer to mathlib standards. Always do the full, proper work — no half-measures, no wrappers around imports, no commented-out code pretending to be a theorem.

before_code:
set_option maxHeartbeats 1600000 in
lemma foo : ... := by
  -- long proof
after_code:
-- Break the proof into helper lemmas so it compiles with default heartbeats
private lemma foo_aux1 : ... := by ...
private lemma foo_aux2 : ... := by ...
lemma foo : ... := by
  exact foo_aux2 (foo_aux1 ...)
description: None of the lemmas should have set_option maxHeartbeats. This means that the proof is too long and needs to be broken up. If you see this you should run the decompose proof agent to break them up.

before_code:
have h1 := foo x
have h2 := bar y
have h3 := baz h1 h2
exact qux h3
description: For the golfing agent: avoid consecutive lines of 'have := ...' in proofs. Trivial haves of the form 'have h := foobar' (no type annotation, just applying a lemma) should be inlined where they are used. Only KEEP haves where the RHS is a nontrivial proof (e.g. 'have h : P := by ...' with actual tactic work). Consecutive trivial haves are a code smell and should be aggressively inlined.

after_code:
-- Only import from within the clean folder:
import LeanModularForms.ValenceFormula.OrbitPairing
import LeanModularForms.ValenceFormula.TextbookExistence
description: When cleaning files into a new folder, NEVER import from outside the clean folder (e.g. no 'import LeanModularForms.Modularforms.valence.ComplexAnalysis.*'). Everything must be moved across lemma by lemma into the clean folder. Clean files should only import from mathlib and from other files within the clean folder. Also, file names must follow mathlib standards (UpperCamelCase.lean, no underscores separating words).

When doing cleaning, only ever work in the new folders you created (LeanModularForms/ValenceFormula/), not going back and modifying the original files (LeanModularForms/Modularforms/valence/ComplexAnalysis/).

The agent should do the following in order every time it runs: 1) Check to see if cleanup has started, if not ask the user what they want to clean. 2) If it has started, check if a migration plan exists. 3) Go through the plan and check that the files are correctly cleaned, if not start cleaning them in order. 4) At the end, update the migration plan with the progress and next steps.

before_code:
def ellipticPoint_i' : UpperHalfPlane := ...
def S_leftVert : Finset UpperHalfPlane := ...
after_code:
def ellipticPointI' : UpperHalfPlane := ...
def sLeftVert : Finset UpperHalfPlane := ...
description: As part of cleanup checks, MUST enforce mathlib naming conventions: defs returning data use lowerCamelCase (no underscores). File names and def/lemma names must all be named to mathlib standards. This is a required check, not a suggestion.
As part of cleanup checks, run the proof decomposer on long proofs (>30 lines) AND run the golf-proof agent. Both are required, not optional.
Make the cleanup plan as parallelisable as possible. When working, launch as many agents in parallel as you can. Structure work so independent pieces run concurrently.

after_code: remove the parameter entirely from signature and call site
description: If you see an unused variable warning, remove the variable entirely from the signature and call sites. Do NOT add a _ prefix.

When launching multiple background agents that edit the SAME file, ALWAYS use isolation: worktree to prevent concurrent file corruption. Without isolation, agents can delete or corrupt files they all touch simultaneously.
Do not start writing new lemmas mid cleanup. Only migrate existing declarations from old chain. Can note opportunities to generalise similar lemmas but do not act on it during migration.

Comprehensive Mathlib-Quality Migrator workflow: (1) Identify main theorem in source file. (2) Create Clean/MainTheorem.lean importing original, restate theorem via simpa using Original.thm. (3) Iterative migration loop: attempt to remove original import, compilation errors become TODO list, for each missing identifier check mathlib first (exact name, near name, structure search, intent search), use mathlib version if found, else copy+clean into Clean/Support.lean. (4) Maintain 3 required files: MIGRATION_PLAN.md (status table with Item/Kind/Action/Mathlib-replacement/Status), MIGRATION_LOG.md (chronological changelog), SIMILAR_LEMMAS.md (groups of near-duplicate lemmas with generalization candidates). (5) Quality standards for copied code: snake_case lemmas, CamelCase types, prefer simp/simpa/aesop over fragile rw chains, minimize typeclass assumptions, generalize where safe. (6) Similar lemmas workflow: when 2-3+ lemmas share proof skeleton, prove general lemma + reprove specifics via simpa using general. (7) Completion: Clean/MainTheorem.lean compiles without importing original, all deps are mathlib or Clean/Support, no obvious duplicates remain. (8) Triage: try mathlib first even if means rewriting statement; if lemma used once prefer inlining; change local statement to match mathlib canonical form.

During migration: (1) Copy proofs across AS-IS first, then break them up into smaller lemmas afterward. Do not try to rewrite proofs during the copy step. (2) NEVER fill sorries during migration unless you are proving a generalisation of a lemma. The goal is to migrate existing proven code, not to do new proof work. If a lemma has a sorry in the original, it keeps the sorry in the clean version. Only introduce new proofs when generalising similar lemmas.

The cleanup is not complete until all proofs have been cleaned AND all results we want to clean don't have sorries left. Style cleanup alone is insufficient — sorry elimination is part of the cleanup plan.
Use the /simplify command where possible to clean up the code. After writing or migrating proofs, run /simplify to review for reuse, quality, and efficiency issues.

before_code:
theorem valenceFormula_gWN_base
theorem valenceFormula_orbit_sum
theorem valenceFormula_orbit_sum_s₀
theorem valenceFormula_textbook_orbit_finsum
after_code:
theorem valence_formula_gWN_base
theorem valence_formula_orbit_sum
theorem valence_formula_orbit_sum_s₀
theorem valence_formula_textbook_orbit_finsum
description: Theorem names with camelCase prefix 'valenceFormula_' renamed to snake_case 'valence_formula_' across 3 files (CoreIdentity, TextbookForm, TextbookExistence). Def names in theorem name prefixes (fdBoundary, normSq, etc.) stay as-is per convention.
Renamed paper reference name 'lemma_3_1_simple_pole' to descriptive mathlib-style 'cauchyPV_sectorCurve_simplePole' (snake_case for theorem returning Prop).

after_code:
private theorem pv_sector_cutoff_base_integrabilities ...
private theorem pv_sector_cutoff_composed_integrabilities ...
theorem pv_sector_cutoff_eq ...
description: Eliminated set_option maxHeartbeats 1600000 by extracting 8 integrability facts into 2 private helper lemmas (base + composed). Pattern: when maxHeartbeats is needed, extract the expensive subgoals into separate lemmas.

after_code:
-- SectorCurve.lean: 675 lines (defs + basic PV)
-- SectorCurveLemma.lean: 924 lines (higher-order results)
description: Split 1568-line SectorCurve.lean at the natural boundary between basic PV computation (pv_sector_dz_over_z) and higher-order results (higher_power, simplePole, negative_power, windingNumber). Both files under 1000 lines.

before_code: rcases le_or_lt t 1 with h1 | h1
after_code: rcases le_or_gt t 1 with h1 | h1
description: Replaced deprecated le_or_lt with le_or_gt (same semantics: a ≤ b ∨ b < a ↔ a ≤ b ∨ a > b). Drop-in replacement.

When decomposing proofs across files, NEVER just move a long proof to another file or parameterize it without also decomposing the body. 'Refactor to eliminate duplication' is NOT the same as 'decompose'. If you create an abstract version of a 900-line proof, that abstract version STILL needs to be decomposed into helpers ≤50 lines. The test is: after ALL changes, is there ANY proof body >50 lines ANYWHERE in the project? If yes, you're not done. Moving a big proof to a shared location is step 1; decomposing it into small helpers is step 2. Never skip step 2.

before_code:
theorem foo
    (S : Finset X)
    (hS : P S)
    (f : A → B) :
    ...
  simp only [ne_eq,
    mul_eq_zero,
    OfNat.ofNat_ne_zero,
    not_false_eq_true]
after_code:
theorem foo (S : Finset X) (hS : P S) (f : A → B) :
    ...
  simp only [ne_eq, mul_eq_zero, OfNat.ofNat_ne_zero, not_false_eq_true]
description: When formatting Lean declarations, ALWAYS maximize use of the 100-character line width. Short hypotheses like (S : Finset X) (hS : P S) (f : A → B) should be grouped on the same line. Long simp only [...] lists should fill lines to ~98 chars before wrapping. Never leave a line at 40 chars when you could fit another hypothesis or simp argument. The test: if two adjacent lines are both under 50 chars, they should almost certainly be merged. This applies to theorem signatures, simp calls, constructor arguments, and any multi-line expression.

before_code:
set_option maxHeartbeats 8000000 in
theorem foo : ... := by
  long_proof
after_code:
private lemma foo_aux1 : ... := by short_proof
private lemma foo_aux2 : ... := by short_proof
theorem foo : ... := by
  exact foo_aux1.trans foo_aux2
description: set_option maxHeartbeats is NEVER acceptable in clean code. When /cleanup encounters it, it MUST either (1) profile the proof to find the slow tactic and golf it, or (2) extract helper lemmas to reduce elaboration cost. Do NOT skip these. Do NOT leave them for later. Every set_option maxHeartbeats must be eliminated during cleanup. This is a mandatory fix, not optional.
